coredata.dc:title: Recent progress and treatment strategy of pectin polysaccharide based tissue engineering scaffolds in cancer therapy, wound healing and cartilage regeneration
coredata.dc:creator: Koshy, Jijo
Sangeetha, D.
coredata.dc:description: Natural polymers and its mixtures in the form of films, sponges and hydrogels are playing a major role in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine. Hydrogels have been extensively investigated as standalone materials for drug delivery purposes as they enable effective encapsulation and sustained release of drugs. Biopolymers are widely utilised in the fabrication of hydrogels due to their safety, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and regulated breakdown by human enzymes. Among all the biopolymers, polysaccharide-based polymer is well suited to overcome the limitations of traditional wound dressing materials. Pectin is a polysaccharide which can be extracted from different plant sources and is used in various pharmaceutical and biomedical applications including cartilage regeneration. Pectin itself cannot be employed as scaffolds for tissue engineering since it decomposes quickly. This article discusses recent research and developments on pectin polysaccharide, including its types, origins, applications, and potential demands for use in AI-mediated scaffolds. It also covers the materials-design process, strategy for implementation to material selection and fabrication methods for evaluation. Finally, we discuss unmet requirements and current obstacles in the development of optimal materials for wound healing and bone-tissue regeneration, as well as emerging strategies in the field.

Soft tissue engineering
Cartilage repair

head.ce:title: Recent progress and treatment strategy of pectin polysaccharide based tissue engineering scaffolds in cancer therapy, wound healing and cartilage regeneration

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore 632014, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore-632014, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural polymers and its mixtures in the form of films, sponges and hydrogels are playing a major role in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine. Hydrogels have been extensively investigated as standalone materials for drug delivery purposes as they enable effective encapsulation and sustained release of drugs. Biopolymers are widely utilised in the fabrication of hydrogels due to their safety, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and regulated breakdown by human enzymes. Among all the biopolymers, polysaccharide-based polymer is well suited to overcome the limitations of traditional wound dressing materials. Pectin is a polysaccharide which can be extracted from different plant sources and is used in various pharmaceutical and biomedical applications including cartilage regeneration. Pectin itself cannot be employed as scaffolds for tissue engineering since it decomposes quickly. This article discusses recent research and developments on pectin polysaccharide, including its types, origins, applications, and potential demands for use in AI-mediated scaffolds. It also covers the materials-design process, strategy for implementation to material selection and fabrication methods for evaluation. Finally, we discuss unmet requirements and current obstacles in the development of optimal materials for wound healing and bone-tissue regeneration, as well as emerging strategies in the field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of biodegradable polymeric materials for biomedical applications has improved dramatically over the previous half-century. Biodegradable polymeric materials are preferred in the development of therapeutic devices such as temporary implants and three-dimensional scaffolds for tissue engineering. More progress has been made in the use of biodegradable polymeric materials for pharmacological applications such as delivery systems for controlled/sustained drug release. These applications necessitate specific physicochemical, biological, and degrading features of the materials in order to provide successful therapy.

Researchers have looked at the potential for wound dressing materials made from both natural and synthetic polymers like polysaccharides, proteoglycans, and proteins because of their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and ability to promote re-epithelialization [1]. The usage of proteins and polysaccharides in a variety of applications is ushering in a new era of rising environmental implications. A paradigm shift in healthcare toward individualised wound care management is steadily occurring with the goal of increasing the efficacy of treatment [2]. Protein and polysaccharide interactions can occur either covalently or non-covalently to produce protein-polysaccharide complexes [3,4]. It was proven that naturally derived protein-polysaccharide complexes contain anti-cancer, immune-regulating, anti-fatigue, and acetylcholinesterase-inhibiting capabilities, among other properties [5–7]. Zhao et al. reported that a polysaccharide-protein combination obtained from a medicinal centipede can decrease tumor growth by enhancing anticancer immune responses [7].

New stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems have been developed in response to the growing emphasis on precision medicine and personalized pharmacotherapy [8–10]. Due to their biocompatibility, mechanical properties, and ease of processing, polymeric scaffolds made of natural polymers (chitosan, pectin, collagen, gelatin, and alginate) and synthetic polymers (poly(lactic acid), poly(D,L-lactic-co-glycolic acid), and poly(L-lactic acid)), have been widely used for a variety of drug delivery applications [11]. Among the drug formulations that have been developed and improved in the fields of drug delivery are hydrogels, films, nanoparticles, and nanocomposites [12–15]. Hydrogels are becoming more famous because of their interesting properties, like being biocompatible and biodegradable, and their unique “soft-wet” nature, which is similar to biological tissue [16,17]. It is well recognized that administering a programmed rate of continuous intravenous infusion is a better method of administering medication since it not only keeps the drug level in the body consistent, therapeutically effective, and prolonged but also prevents hepatic “first-pass” exclusion. [18]. Drug concentration can be controlled and is directly delivered into the bloodstream via intravenous infusion [19]. However, this type of drug delivery demands hospitalization for the patient and some medical regulation of the drug. To distribute systemic medication instead, various transdermal drug delivery devices (TDDS) have been created and applied to the skin surface [20].

Tissue and organ failure resulting from defects, injuries, or other forms of damage is one of the most debilitating and expensive health care issues. Various surgical strategies have been devised to address these issues, including total artificial substitutes like joint prostheses and artificial kidneys, non-living processed tissues like heart valves, and autogenic or allogenic tissues (transplantation). The new field of tissue engineering combines the scientific disciplines of biology, materials science, and biomedical engineering to restore and replace failing biological tissues and organs over the long term. Strategies for cell-based tissue engineering create intriguing new opportunities that may be applicable to a wide range of clinical applications. There are currently no clinically acceptable solutions for cartilage tissue regeneration [21,22]. The most common clinical treatment for treating cartilage damage entails drilling a hole through the injured cartilage to the subchondral bone, allowing blood to flow and new biological material to enter [23–25]. However, these clinical procedures frequently lead to the development of fibrocartilaginous tissue, which is weaker than the original tissue, fails to integrate effectively with surrounding tissue, and degrades over a period of six to twelve months [26–29]. Hydrogels have emerged as promising candidates for utilization in the field of cartilage regeneration. Hydrogels are polymeric networks composed of water-soluble polymers, which adopt a three-dimensional structure. These networks are crosslinked through either covalent or physical means[30], resulting in the formation of a hydrogel that is insoluble in water [31,32].

Wound dressings are intended to support the wound bed and protect it from elements that may delay or hinder healing, such as contamination and moisture loss, hence encouraging and accelerating recovery. Wound which commonly occurs in daily life, in car accidents, and in fires, needs quick medical attention since the wounded skin loses its ability to act as an infection barrier [33]. For wound healing, a variety of synthetic medications and artificial skin patches are commonly used [34,35]. However, these are costly and can result in adverse responses and drug resistance. As a result, other medications and new treatment strategies are required [36]. Wound dressings are classified according to the type of injury: passive, interactive, advanced, and bioactive. Passive wound dressings, like gauze, absorbent pads, and bandages, protect the wound site from bacterial penetration and mechanical impact but are unable to control the amount of moisture present in the wound. These kinds of covers are helpful for small wounds. Due to their flexibility, interactive dressings (hydrogels consisting of polyurethanes and translucent silicone film or foam) are excellent for wounds in joints but difficult to access in other areas of the body. By managing the moisture in the wound environment, modern dressings that include alginates, hydrofibres, and hydrocolloids promote wound healing. As the next generation of wound healing products, bioactive wound dressings (such as skin grafts or replacements, drug-loaded, and antimicrobial dressings) have been developed as a consequence of recent investigations. The resultant dressings successfully speed up wound healing and increase dressing durability [37–39]. Furthermore, by providing therapeutic ingredients in a controlled manner, these multipurpose wound dressings are essential for accelerating wound healing [40,41].

In addition to maintaining a moist environment and being biocompatible and mechanically flexible, an effective wound dressing should also have the following properties: permeability, exudate absorption, which provides effective defense against bacterial infections and external stress, easy removal without adhesion [41]. Among the several forms of wounds, chronic wounds are widely known to cause inadequate skin regeneration with certain materials, transmit harm to the muscle, tendon, and bone, and as a result burden people economically. Despite the wide variety of wound dressings that are currently available, improving the quality and dependability of wound dressings is absolutely necessary [42,43]. Here, we discuss the widespread application of pectin polysaccharides and the fabrication techniques for scaffolds. This manuscript's primary body covers polymeric scaffold-based tissue engineering investigations on wound dressing, bone, and cartilage regeneration. The concluding section of the review consists of a detailed and critical analysis of the currently available techniques for the preparation of polymeric scaffolds from pectin, as well as a discussion of the main obstacles facing the current fabrication of materials and its future trends.

The global issue of plastic pollution in both aquatic and terrestrial environment is a matter of concern. There exists a significant amount of research indicating that the presence of plastics within the ecosystems are steadily increasing and has detrimental effects on animals [44–51]. The pollution of soil and various aquatic ecosystems continues to be a significant environmental concern attributed to the expansion of the oil and gas sector. The contamination of the environment by radioecological elements poses a significant threat to the well-being of biological systems and the overall ecosystem, impeding its growth and development. [52]. Efforts aimed at mitigating plastic waste and its subsequent pollution in aquatic and terrestrial habitats have been pursued for several decades. For example, the European Union (EU) has implemented Waste Framework Directives since 1975, which have developed a waste management hierarchy [53]. It encompasses many management strategies, ranked in order of preference from most beneficial to least favourable. These practices include prevention, reuse, recycling, recovery, and disposal [54]. Fig. 1 illustrates the recycling process of plastic waste as a means of mitigating environmental pollution.

New technologies aim to suit the needs of consumers and industries. Changes in food production, sales practises, and consumer lifestyles, along with environmental consciousness and the development of new fields of knowledge (such as nanotechnology and biotechnology), are the driving forces behind the development of smart packaging that can extend the shelf life of food while maintaining and monitoring its safety and quality, as well as protecting the environment. Biodegradable polymer manufacturing incorporates extra processes that do not affect the polymers' biodegradability. In commercial terms, they may be synthetic (chemical) or biotechnology (effected by enzymes or microorganisms). Biopolymers are made from renewable resources. Bioplastics provide environmental benefits such as recyclability and reusability when compared to traditional petroleum-based synthetic polymers. Plastics are materials that, when handled under certain conditions, can be molded or deformed. In contrast to conventional plastics made from fossil fuels, bioplastics are biodegradable and generated from renewable resources [55]. Sustainable materials often come from crops, meaning they are not harmful to the environment or people. Since the beginning of time, scientists have been looking for sustainable and efficient materials to treat wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages of biomaterials in biomedical applications
ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of sustainable biomedical technology depends not only on the development of new principles to surmount technological obstacles, but also on the appropriate consideration of social and environmental concerns, particularly in the mass production of viable materials. In this context, it is essential to reduce the use of hazardous compounds and increase the use of environmentally sustainable “green” materials in the production of nanoscale-based composite materials through the use of transparent and environmentally friendly chemical synthesis routes [56–58]. Liposomes, nanocapsules, nanoparticles, polymeric micelles, nano-structured lipid carriers, inorganic nanoparticles, and solid lipid nanoparticles have emerged as prominent nanomaterials in contemporary scientific research [56,57]. The advantages of the nanomaterials include improved surface reactivity, increased colloidal stability, and enhanced dispersibility. However, most researchers are now attempting to transition toward ecologically safe, cost-effective biodegradable, non-allergenic, biocompatible, and non-toxic materials of biological origin. In this regard, a wide number of biomaterials, both organic and inorganic, have been researched as essential scaffolds in a variety of formats, including bioceramics, nanocomposites, bio composites, nanofibers, and hydrogels for a range of biomedical and healthcare applications [59–61]. Designing scaffolds for effective development in biomedical sectors has attracted a lot of interest due to the availability and abundance of bio-polymeric materials as well as the pervasive therapeutic need for safe scaffolds.

Any natural or synthetic substance intended to interact with biological processes in order to influence medical therapy is referred to as a biomaterial [62]. Biomaterials must be biocompatible, which means they must carry out their function with an appropriate host response [63]. Extensive research has been conducted to develop materials that cater to the requirements of the biomedical community, encompassing a wide range of compositions including metals, ceramics, glasses, and polymers. Polymers exhibit considerable promise due to their inherent chemical flexibility, which engenders a wide array of physical and mechanical characteristics in materials. The ability of biomaterials to degrade, excrete, or be reabsorbed without removal or surgical revision makes degradable polymers of paramount importance. This research aims to analyse the application of pectin-based biomaterials as a potential alternative therapeutic strategy for replacing existing treatment choices.

Biopolymers can be categorized into two distinct types: non-biodegradable and biodegradable biopolymers. These materials can be classified as biopolymers, derived from both fossil fuel and natural sources, based on their attributed origins. Numerous studies have been done to propose novel biodegradable packaging materials and polymers derived from renewable resources, with a specific focus on finding alternatives to petroleum-based materials. These efforts have been driven by the need to address environmental concerns and have entailed the implementation of specific measures. The utilization of biopolymer-based materials has the potential to significantly address waste disposal challenges. However, in some instances, their commercial application is limited by weak mechanical and physical qualities, as well as inadequate gas and water vapour barrier properties, when compared to commonly used petroleum-derived plastic materials. As a result, numerous studies have been conducted to advance novel techniques designed to enhance the mechanical and barrier features of biopolymers, along with the integration of bioactive chemicals to give antioxidant and antibacterial properties that are essential in bio-based applications [64,65].

Biopolymers can be classified into three categories, namely natural, microbial, and synthetic biopolymers, which are distinguished based on the sources of their raw materials. Natural biopolymers can be categorized based on their source (flora or fauna) and can be further classified into polysaccharides-based biopolymers, which are derived from cellulose, starch, algae, chitosan, alginate, and other similar sources, and proteins-based biopolymers, which encompass gelatin, gluten, soy protein, albumin, elastin, fibrin, alginates, microfibrillar, collagen, and keratin. These biopolymers are primarily obtained from biomass. The polymer classification from polysaccharides, Lipids and proteins are described in Fig. 2 .

Pectin is a branching heteropolysaccharide composed of galacturonan segments and neutral sugars like rhamnose, arabinose, galactose, and xylose. It helps to the cell structure by forming a matrix with cellulose and hemicellulose. Pectins are hydrophilic polysaccharides derived from the cell membranes of plants. They comprise linear chains of α-D-galacturonic acid residues that are (1 → 4)-linked. Part of the carboxylic group in the galacturonic chain exists in the methyl ester form; the degree of methylation, which is expressed as a percentage of the esterified carboxyl groups, is an essential means to classify pectins. Fig. 3 represents the structure of pectin. The primary cell wall gives plants their structural integrity, imparts flexibility to the tissue, and controls growth in response to cell turgor pressure. Crystalline cellulose microfibrils (CMFs), a component of primordial cell walls, are enmeshed in a matrix of pectin, xyloglucan, and endogenous proteins. The primary cell wall's overall mechanical characteristics are controlled by the structural characteristics of these parts and how they interact with one another [66,67]. Primary cell wall is also a material source for a range of products. In recent years, pectin has been employed as an ingredient in the food industry as well as in the pharmaceutical and medicinal fields [68].Recently, edible films or coatings were envisioned as a novel application for pectin polymers. These films protect fruits and vegetables from microbial contamination by acting as a natural barrier to the exchange of moisture, gases, lipids, and volatiles between food and environment [69]. Fig. 4 represents the number of research work carried out in the last 10 years. Citrus and apple pectins are used in commercial pectins. Apple pomace contains 10-15 % pectin by drymass, but citrus peel contains 20-30 % pectin by drymass. Pectin has also been extracted in greater quantities from a variety of other fruits and by-products, including sunflower heads, mango peels, banana peels, passion fruit peels, sugar beet pulp, soy bean hulls, Akebia trifoliata peels, peach peels, chickpea husks, and so on[69]. Fig. 5 depicts the extraction of pectin from plant sources and its method. Here are some lists based on their yield and extraction methods such as MAE, UAE, EAE & UMAE are as follows (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical properties of pectin and its extraction
ce:section.ce:para: Pectin's diverse properties and performances are determined by its chemical makeup and structure. Gelling ability is one of the most important aspects [88–90]. The structural investigation of pectin found that it is a polymer composed of a chain-like configuration of 100-1000 saccharide units; thus, it lacks a definite structure [69]. The gel formation is aided by hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, and/or ionic connections between different hydroxyl groups and free, methylated, or amidated carboxyl groups [91,92].

Emerging technologies such as ultrasound, microwave, radiofrequency, electromagnetic, enzyme-assisted, and deep eutectic solvents extraction have been utilised in order to obtain pectin from a variety of feedstocks with their original chemical structure and composition and to prevent environmental contamination. Extraction methods that are assisted by newly emerging technologies have been shown to be more effective than more conventional extraction methods. Both the application of external energy or stimuli and the enhancement of chemical reaction rates can be classified as extraction techniques. These techniques can be divided into two categories as follows: the first category is based on the application of external energy/stimuli, and the second category is based on the enhancement of chemical reaction rates [88].

Pectin can be combined with a variety of polymers, both natural and synthetic, as well as other chemicals. The mixing of pectin with various materials has extended its possibilities for future prospective. Pectin-derived materials have numerous applications; some of the most relevant features are discussed (Fig. 6 ).

In the case of pectin, it is only possible to generate Ca-dependent gels within a specific concentration range, as pectin cannot dissolve fully at extremely high concentrations and gelation does not occur at low concentrations [93]. At low concentrations, pectin tends to produce Calcium-mediated intrachain crosslinks that do not contribute to gel mechanical strength. [94]. Pectin as a biopolymer is used in packaging and as a carrier molecule for antimicrobials, antioxidants, and other related compounds in the food preservation domain. Growing concerns about food safety, as well as a demand for natural and eco-friendly technologies, have shifted research interests toward the use of sustainable materials in food packaging [95].

In the food industry, pectin is primarily used to produce edible coatings and packaging materials. Because of its ability to solidify into gels and films, it has found application in the manufacturing of biodegradable food packaging as well as edible coatings for the purpose of extending the shelf life of food [96,97]. The use of edible coatings and food packaging made from materials derived from sources of petroleum is connected to the depletion of natural resources. In contrast, pectin coatings have been the subject of research in recent years for their potential to lengthen the period of time that food products can be stored at room temperature. This is primarily due to the fact that pectin coatings are renewable, biodegradable, and biocompatible. These characteristics are connected to the 12 green chemistry principles, and reaching a sustainable world requires a strong emphasis on their implementation. In the food products, the coating has a significant effect to control the loss of water and to reduce decay in fruits, which helps to maintain their firmness and extends the shelf life of the products [98].

Every year, food packaging generates millions of tonnes of waste in landfills, having a devastating effect on the environment [99]. In this way, rising safety concerns and the demand for natural and ecological technologies have prompted research into sustainable materials for food packaging [96,100]. Pectin coatings that are edible alter the atmosphere surrounding the fruit, altering the oxygen levels within the fruit and thereby preventing physiological decomposition. In addition, it reduces the deterioration of fruit quality caused by ripening in terms of texture or loss of bioactive compounds during storage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blending of different matrix resulting bio polymers
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the presence of uronic acid in its structure, pectin derived from a variety of natural sources possesses antimicrobial properties that can be enhanced by introducing favourable structural modifications. By substituting, elongating, and depolymerizing pectin chains with natural functional compounds, one can achieve the desired structural change. Through ionic, hydrophobic, hydrogen-bonding, and weak van der Waals forces, the interaction between pectin and these substances is strengthened [101]. Pectin, in conjunction with chitosan, was used as part of a nano-multilayer coating. The whole ‘Tommy Atkins’ mangoes were treated with this nano-multilayer system, which prevented them from rotting. In comparison to coated mangoes, uncoated mangoes lost more weight, had more total soluble solids, and had lower titratable acidity. Chitosan's antimicrobial and gas barrier properties, as well as pectin's low oxygen permeability, contributed to mangoes having a longer shelf life [102].

Bio- materials have a significant role in extending the shelf life of food and helping to minimise the rapid spoilage of food on contact with the external environment. However, some packaging material containing toxins directly affects the food and increases the food preservation rate; and consumers pay much attention on buying those things. Bio packaging, often known as smart packaging, is a new revolutionary solution that allows consumers to buy food in a more informed manner. It's a new concept in the realm of packaging, and it's based on the synergistic impact of both active (antimicrobial and antioxidant) as well as intelligent materials (showing product freshness, temperature, and safety) that are incorporated into the package. Nowadays, packaging-based products promoting the shelf life of food more effectively. For food packaging, synthetic plastics are commonly utilised because of their strong mechanical stress resistance, high barrier characteristics, molding flexibility, and low cost. Plastics take years to decompose and may damage food quality by releasing chemicals, therefore their use in food packaging has been heavily criticized. Petroleum resources are also finite, and as a result, future costs are anticipated to grow [103].

Ingestion of food contaminated with bacteria, viruses, parasites, or chemical compounds such as heavy metals results in over 200 diseases. This escalating public health issue has significant socioeconomic effects, including strains on health-care systems, decreased productivity, and harm to tourism and commerce. These diseases considerably contribute to the worldwide disease and mortality burden. In today's world, petrochemical-based plastics such as polyamide (PA), polypropylene (PP), polyethylene (PE), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyvinylchloride (PVC), and polystyrene (PS) have been widely used as packaging materials due to their low cost. However, their use is currently restricted due to their inability to degrade, causing major environmental problems [104]. As a result, researchers discovered a way to alleviate these problems by converting waste plastic into usable resources through recycling. It is, nevertheless, a tremendous operation to transform food packaging materials into usable items. Despite the fact that packing films based of carbohydrate and protein are typically excellent oxygen hinderers at moderate to low humidity and frequently have strong mechanical features, these films are ineffective as a water vapour barrier due to their hydrophilic properties. The mechanical, thermal, and physical properties of bio-based polymer packaging films are still unsatisfactory, and they are often difficult to use in industrial applications despite a significant increase in research efforts aimed at improving the properties of composite materials [105]. The majority of studies shows that adding fillers (organic or inorganic) enhances the biopolymer matrix's overall properties.

Polymers can be utilised as sensors in the packaging industry to accurately assess whether food is spoiled or not. Additionally, intelligent packaging monitors the shelf life, safety, and freshness of food, as well as the storage temperature, pH, and gas/water vapour permeability [106]. This indicator changes colour in response to variations in food pH, allowing customers to tell the difference between fresh and rotten food without having to open the packaging [106]. Since 2010, there has been an increase in the number of articles that are related to food freshness. These articles demonstrate the growing research interest on food freshness detection. As a result of the gradually rising number of publications on the topic, there is a growing interest among researchers in food freshness sensors. Furthermore, there are a number of articles that have been examined in relation to food freshness sensors. There are lot of articles cover topics such as the development of biosensors, the fabrication of electrochemical sensors utilising a variety of different types of indicators [107–109]. Freshness sensors are defined as sensors that can detect changes in pH, temperature, relative humidity, and colour [110]. In addition, a sensor for food freshness is sensitive to any changes that occur during food degradation, such as colour change, gas produced by food spoilage, and bacterial count [111].

Electrochemical sensors are devices that are used to study chemical reactions between analyte and electrode material. These sensors are characterised by their ability to analyse electrical signals. Pectin has been shown to be effective as a stabiliser for the colloidal nanoparticles, which are necessary for the immobilisation of enzymes in many different situations. For the purpose of detecting glucose, for instance, Thakur et al. synthesized nanoparticles of polyaniline (PAni) by combining pectin and immobilised glucose oxidase enzyme. Due to increased homogeneity and higher functionalization of PAni nanoparticles with the enzyme, the as-synthesized electrode GOx-PAni-Pec nanoparticles exhibited high specificity. In this case, pectin improved the stability of the biosensor by encasing the PAni in its gelatinous structure. This gelatinous structure provides a porous texture, which enables the simple transport of analyte through it and reduces the amount of enzyme that is leached away. This biosensor demonstrated a low detection threshold of 43.5 M despite having a wide linear range that extended from 0.06 mM to 43.5 M.

Pectin is used as a stable polymeric substance to stabilise nanoparticles [112]. Several strategies are employed to encapsulate nanomaterials within the pectin polymer in order to prevent aggregation. Nanomaterials based on pectin have demonstrated a variety of environmental applications. Due to its variable viscoelasticity, pectin has been combined with polypropylene and reduced graphene oxide (PPy/rGO) electrode material to improve electrochemical performance. Pectin has been found to induce amorphous and hydrophilic properties in conducting polymer matrices, thereby enhancing the ionic and molecular diffusion of analytes [113].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pectin based materials for removal of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: In the food chain, heavy metal pollution has been viewed as a serious pollution problem, but it cannot be degraded or decomposed due to the bio-concentration. As an example, Pb2+ toxicity affected the nervous system, bone marrow hematopoietic function, digestive system, and reproductive organs. As a result, the removal of Pb2+ has received considerable attention [114–119]. In addition, other heavy metal ions, such as Cu2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, and Ag+, have been studied [120]. To remove heavy metal ions from the sewage, pectin is an ideal choice because of its properties. Native pectin, modified pectin-based adsorbents, and physically treated pectin-based adsorbents are the most common types of pectin-based materials used in heavy metal removal [120].

The higher the number of active sites, the better it is for the coordination reaction and, consequently, for the adsorption capacity. Electron-rich functional groups can be introduced into pectin by means of modifications such as ionically crosslinking and hydrolysis as well as esterification, amination, and free radical reaction [120]. The initial concentration of pollutants, pH values, and adsorbent dosages all had an impact on adsorption performance. It is one of the most important driving forces of adsorption to have a higher initial concentration, which in turn led to higher adsorption capacity [121]. An increase in adsorbent dosage can also increase its ability to absorb, while too much dosage of the adsorbents can lead to inter-particle interactions (e.g., aggregation) that reduce the accessible adsorbent surface area, fail to utilise all effective adsorption sites, and lengthen the diffusional path [114]. Pectin's ability to bind metals was greatly affected by the pH of the solution. When the pH of the solution was below Gal A's pKa, the carboxyl groups were -COOH; as the pH increased, the carboxyl groups dissociated to -COO-. As a result, the homogeneity of active sites was affected by the solution pH, resulting in noticeable differences in adsorption performance [122]. To capture heavy metal ions, the thermal motion of molecular molecules can be affected by temperature [123]. The adsorption capacity rises with increasing temperature in the low-temperature range. Heavy metal ions exit the surface of the adsorbent at higher temperatures due to increased thermal motion, which shows the decrease in capacity for absorption [124].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pectin based polymer for wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: It is now widely accepted that bioactive components in dressings can influence physiological processes and help wounds heal more quickly and effectively. Extracellular matrix proteins can easily migrate and growth factors are more active and available in a wet environment, which enhances healing [125,126]. In addition to being biocompatible, biodegradable, and non-cytotoxic, pectin occurs naturally as a hydrocolloid and is thus suitable for use in wound healing applications. Due to the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carboxymethyl groups on the backbone chain, it can be readily functionalized and modified. Pectin and its derivatives are regarded as highly suitable for use in wound healing. The high concentration of esterified galacturonic acid residues in pectin is responsible for its potent anti-inflammatory effect, which suppresses inflammatory enzymes including iNOS and COX-2. For the treatment of wounds, chemically sulfated citrus pectin fractions with potent antithrombogenic properties have been developed [127]. Even in the absence of heparin co-factor II, sulphated pectins inhibited athrombin directly and were 100 % effective against venous thrombosis. In the first four days when papin was immobilised in pectin and used for wound debridement, 20 % faster healing was observed [128]. Pectin is a naturally occurring anti-glycation agent that has proven effective in treating impaired wound healing. Electrospraying was used to develop aerosols of collagen-pectin microparticles for the treatment of diabetic wounds and lung regeneration [129].

Pectin may be one of the most acceptable feedstocks that can be utilised in the drug delivery application due to its adaptable modification strategies and anti-cancer bioactivity. In addition, pectin possesses a unique gelling mechanism in acidic conditions, microbiological breakdown throughout the digestive tract, and biocompatibility. Pectin-based medication administration by ionically cross-linking techniques has been documented since 1946. When calcium salts are added to a pectin-containing aqueous solution, a coordination complex is generated between the pectin chains and Ca2+, resulting in the formation of a gel network in the solution. With increasing quantities of pectin and/or metal ions, the number of pectin fractal flocs increased concurrently with the crosslinking density, resulting in a rise in elastic modulus values [130]. These performance enhancements allowed for the development of medication delivery applications.

The “one-pot” method of mixing pectin and calcium salt in solution was the primary method for preparing pectin-based products with micro- and nano-structure, including film, bulk, and tablets. Increasing the concentration of Ca2+ as a cross-linking agent could dramatically increase the maximum viscosity of amidated pectin gels. However, the viscosity of the gel reduced when more Ca2+ was added. In addition, drug release was accelerated for pectin with a lower level of amidation in the presence of calcium, which may have been due to matrix disintegration [131]. Droplets containing pectin and drug were added to calcium-ion-containing solutions in the “two-steps” method. Microbead size was found to be influenced by pectin type, stirring rate, and cross-linking time.[132]. It has been demonstrated that the drug release pattern exhibited by calcium pectinate beads can be broken down into two distinct stages. (1) solvent was able to penetrate the calcium pectinate network, which resulted in swelling; (2) ion exchange between calcium and potassium and/or sodium ions derived from the dissolution medium contributed to the subsequent disintegration of the substance [133].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of green nanomaterials in drug delivery
ce:section.ce:para: The field of nanomedicine and nanoparticulate drug delivery systems is currently experiencing remarkable advancements due to their significant contributions to the advancement of therapeutic approaches for diverse forms of cancer and malignancies. The development of tissue engineering scaffolds, cancer nanotherapy, imaging, and management all depend on the use of nanostructures and biopolymers made from natural and biorenewable resources such proteins, lipids, lignin, hyaluronic acid, starch, cellulose, gum, pectin, alginate, and chitosan [134]. In order to reduce by-products and prepare eco-friendly and safe nano-assemblies to address pressing issues affecting the environment or human health, green nanotechnology incorporates green chemistry principles that call for the use of natural and renewable feedstocks, less hazardous resources, and benign solvents [135,136]. The development of green nanomedicine as one of the most recent approaches to tumor drug delivery and cancer nanotherapy is currently underway. Green nanomedicine is an emerging discipline that combines green nanotechnology with the pharmaceutical and biomedical sciences for the production and development of nano particle-based therapeutics [137–141].

Due to its chemical properties, pectin can interact to build intricate networks that can withstand pressure while protecting and releasing the beneficial substances that are nanoencapsulated inside throughout the digestive tract [142,143]. The properties attributed to pectin as bio-adhesiveness and low toxicity have stimulated researchers to investigate the use of pectin as a material for coating structures on the nanometer-scale to encapsulate bioactive compounds, mainly for the transport and the controlled release in the intestine [144,145]. Pectins, either in isolation or in conjunction with other biopolymers such as polysaccharides or proteins, have the ability to form nanostructures that facilitate the targeted release of bioactive chemicals. The pectin-derived nanoparticles undergo swelling within the gastrointestinal tract. Upon initial exposure to intestinal bacteria, the process of pectin metabolization (degradation) takes place, leading to the progressive release of encapsulated chemicals. This release happens with minimal chemical alteration, resulting in passive absorption inside the colon [146]. However, intestinal cells can identify the glycosidic structures of nanocompounds. Endosomes can transport nanostructures into the cytoplasm, enhancing the absorption and consequent bioavailability of the encapsulated bioactive compounds [147].

The composition and structure of the sugar and other conjugated biomacromolecules, the nature and charge of the encapsulated bioactive molecules, the nanoparticle geometry, and the surface orientation all have a role in the release of nano encapsulated bioactive chemicals [148]. Using pectin-whey protein complex, Ghasemi et al. [149] successfully encapsulated a bioactive compound extracted from orange peel. The incorporation of protein within the nanostructures resulted in increased stability and controlled release kinetics. The characteristics of hydrogels and nanoparticles are synergistically manifested in nanogels composed of pectin, resulting in a novel class of materials known as pectin-based nanohydrogels [150]. Various approaches are utilised in the development of pectin-based nanogels, including self-assembly, physical processing, ionic gelation, and emulsification. The utilization of pectin-based nanogel offers several benefits attributed to the distinct biological characteristics of other biopolymers. These advantages include enhanced chemical stability, which facilitates improved bioavailability and permeability of encapsulated drugs in comparison to their standalone counterparts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pectin derived carbon dots forms and aerogels for environmental applications
ce:section.ce:para: Citrus pectin is a naturally occurring polysaccharide composed of (1-4) linked α-d-galacturonic acid backbones with a large number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, and is widely used in the food and biomedical industries [151–153]. Citrus pectin is easily and abundantly obtainable from citrus pomace, an industrial by-product of citrus [154]. Notably, a large quantity of citrus pomace is typically discarded as waste, causing environmental issues [154]. Consideration is being given to synthesising carbon microspheres using citrus pectin as a precursor for waste utilization.

Since the discovery of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles in 2004. CDs have garnered a significant amount of attention. In contrast to semiconductor quantum dots, which are known to be toxic and may pose a threat to the environment [155,156], CDs have a number of enticing qualities, including low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility for the purpose of fluorescent labelling. To synthesize blue fluorescence C-dots, heating pectin solution in an alkaline medium. The preparation method of blue C-dots they prepared is different from other protocols that require pectin hydrogel and hydrothermal treatment. C-dot formation and fluorescent behaviour were examined and the literature on carbohydrate carbonization suggests a mechanism for the formation of C-dots from pectin [157].

The formation of foam involves entrapping air bubbles in a liquid film, semi-solid, or solid substance. This results in the formation of a dispersion that is spatially homogeneous. When producing aerated food materials like ice cream with a high overrun, foaming properties like capacity and stability play an important role in the production process. It is possible that increasing the concentration of eggplant pectin will result in a significant improvement in the foaming properties [158]. A combination of higher phenolic compound concentrations and lower surface tension may explain the improved foaming capacity and stability, they speculated [159]. Maximum foam volume of the resultant pectin was found in the pH range of 2.0 to 6.0, possibly due to the presence of more protein moiety, which facilitates rapid adsorption at the air-water interface to reduce surface tension. Due to conformation and surface changes of pectin molecules, the foaming capacity was found to be diminished in an alkaline pH (= 8.0) environment [160]. Pectin's and gums, on the other hand, are not considered to be true surfactants; rather, they can be utilised to make the solution more viscous. This was discovered during earlier research. Because of this thickening property, the coalescence of gas bubbles can be significantly slowed down, leading to improved foaming stability [161,162].

Based on Chen et al.'s studies, biodegradable, foam-like materials made from regenerative pectin and sodium montmorillonite clay were made using a quick, eco-friendly freeze-drying procedure. The polymer appeared to be cross-linked after the multivalent cations (Ca2+ and Al3+) were added, improving the aerogel's characteristics. The findings showed that pectin aerogels have better rates of effective biodegradation than wheat starch, which is frequently used as a benchmark. Surprisingly, the biodegradation rates were boosted by the addition of clay and multivalent cations [163].

Due to the presence of polar groups, pectin is hydrophilic by nature and readily absorbs significant amounts of water to form a viscous solution, giving it the capacity to gel. It is employed in the production of jelly, fruit jam, fruit juice, and other foods because of its capability [164]. HMP, LMP, and amidated LMP are the three most common commercial pectins [165]. Among these, HMP is typically used to make jams and jellies with a lot of sugar, mostly by producing heat-irreversible gel after cooling at high temperatures. High DM of HMP, however, makes it less hydrophilic and more susceptible to elimination. Since LMP typically forms thermally reversible gel at a lower temperature, LMP with superior hydrophilicity and more acid stability is extensively employed. Because of its improved gelling capabilities, amidated LMP is now one of the most widely used commercial pectin derivatives. According to Feng et al., an ammonia reaction was used to generate a series of pectins with varying degrees of amidation (DA), and their structural properties, including neutral sugar concentrations, molecular weight contribution, and molecular shape, were thoroughly determined. The findings showed that low pH and a sufficient amount of amide groups are required for the acid-induced gelation of amidated pectin. When DA was <35.36 %, the amide group concentration in pectin chains was insufficient to create a gel network. The gelation process was dramatically accelerated as DA increased [166].

The conventional pectin delivery mechanism would only be triggered progressively by bacterial enzymes in the colon. However, the high-amidated pectin investigated in the study was able to simultaneously satisfy the stable protection of extremely delicate ingredients under gastric acid and the rapid release in the intestinal environment, suggesting that amidation-modified pectins might represent a difficult area that offers a lot of opportunities for the pharmaceutical industry. Additionally, highly amidated pectin may have the potential to become a popularly utilised innovative functional porous material with high pH sensitivity in the future due to its renewability, biodegradability, and low toxicity when used to create aerogels [165–167].

Rheology is the primary testing method providing researchers with a powerful instrument for investigating the viscoelasticity of gels [168]. It is required to briefly provide the fundamental understanding of which hydrogel qualities can be measured using rheology with the help of some rheological traits. The basic technique for quantitatively evaluating natural biopolymer hydrogels is the small deformation rheology experiment. Shi et al. observed that normal pectin had the maximum apparent viscosity, which tended to decrease as ultrasonic intensity and time were increased. The long-chained original PEP (Premna microphylla Turcz) molecules were entangled with one another to form a cross-linked structure, which demonstrated a high apparent viscosity. This may be the cause of these results. The PEP chains got shorter as ultrasonic intensity and exposure time increased, resulting in fewer cross-linked structures and a decrease in apparent viscosity [169]. According to Wang et al., throughout the analysed shear-rate range, untreated pectin had higher apparent viscosity and more significant shear-thinning flow than that of ultrasound-treated pectins, particularly at low shear rates. These results may be due to the fact that untreated pectin had bigger Mw and longer flexible chains than ultrasound-treated pectin, which indicated that there were more intermolecular entanglements and interactions between polymer chains, leading to a substantial increase in viscosity [170].

One of the most important applications of pectin is for emulsifying and minimizing interfacial tension at the oil-water contact. Since pectin is a hydrophilic polymer, the hydrophobicity of the protein, feruloyl, and acetyl moieties has a significant impact on the emulsifying abilities of pectin. A good emulsifier should be able to completely encircle and adsorb itself at the oil-water interface while simultaneously providing appropriate protection against flocculation and coalescence. The emulsification characteristics of pectin are provided by the presence of surface-active molecules. Pectin can also be useful in the creation of emulsions because it can lower the interfacial tension between an oil phase and a water phase [171]. Three main processes by which the pectin produces emulsification are steric stabilisation, electrostatic stabilisation, and raising the viscosity of the continuous phase. According to Zioga et al., all emulsions showed a shear thinning behaviour because the intermolecular strength of the pectin gradually decreased during the testing. According to the research, the more stable emulsions were those with a higher viscosity and a lower conductivity [172].

The use of emulsifiers and stabilisers allows for the formulation and stability of emulsions. Several polysaccharides, including pectin, can function effectively as emulsifying agents even though proteins are the most frequently employed emulsifiers. Pectins contribute to the stability of emulsions by making the continuous aqueous phase of the emulsion more viscous and so slowing down the movement of the oil droplets [173]. Pectins may serve as stabilisers as well as emulsifiers, according to studies, which also notes that pectins from sugar beetroot, pumpkin, citrus, hawhorn and okra have good emulsifying qualities [174]. A variety of parameters influence their emulsifying ability, including DE, protein content, acetyl group content (as reflected by DA, i.e., degree of acetylation), presence of ferrulic acid, side chain branching, molecular weight, as well as concentration, ionic strength, and pH. The emulsifying characteristics of pectin are enhanced by its high protein and acetyl group concentrations as well as its extensive branching [175].

In addition to nanocomposites, nanoemulsions (NEs) with an oily core and a surfactant shell [176,177] have also been developed for use in wound healing [178]. After topical administration, lipophilic substances can be delivered to the superficial stratum corneum using NEs that incorporate poorly water-soluble medicines in the oil droplet phase. [179,180]. These systems must be enclosed in a secondary structure, such as a polymeric scaffold, as their viscosity is insufficient for direct application to the skin [181]. One of the most promising treatments for wound healing is hydrogel-based nanocomposites because they encourage skin adhesion while maintaining a moist and cold environment [182].

Pectins are anionic, soluble, non-starch polysaccharides extracted from the primary cell walls of plants [183]. The ζ-potential is a parameter linked to the charge density of pectin polymers that has been shown to be substantially impacted by both intrinsic and external factors such as polymer structural properties [184] and pH of the medium, respectively. Due to the presence of carboxylic groups, which can be negatively charged depending on the pH of the medium, pectin is regarded in this context as a polyelectrolyte [184]. Opanasopit et al. reported that dissolved pectin is having negatively charge at neutral pH and approaches zero charge at low pH. Pectin has a pKa value of roughly 3.5. Therefore, the formulation's pH may have an impact on the pectin molecule's level of ionisation and its electrostatic interaction with cations. According to the study, as pH rose from 2 to 5.5, the zeta potential for a 0.1 % pectin solution climbed from 4 to 54 mV; the maximum zeta potential was at pH 5.5. High pH 5 is known to cause high methoxy pectin to dissolve quickly [183].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Main challenges in the development of pectin as biopolymer
ce:section.ce:para: There has been a great deal of progress in pectin's extraction methods, structural modifications, and applications since its discovery in 1825. To aid in the development of subsequent industrial applications, researchers in this field have been utilising the extraction optimization process [185–190]. Pectins, which are increasingly important and widely used in the food and pharmaceutical industries, as well as in biomedical applications, are being studied more and more [191]. Stability and ideal molecular weight are still unanswered questions, as are interactions with other compounds. Pectin films have been extensively studied in the food industry, but few studies have examined their use in meat products, which may be due to the fact that pectin films are hydrophilic and easily dissolve on meat products [192]. Consequently, this is a challenge that needs to be overcome if this film can be used in a broader range of foodstuffs.

Due to the presence of numerous hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbomethoxy, and acylamino functional groups, pectin is capable of generating a wide range of derivatives. In the past few decades, pectin properties such as hydration, swelling, and erosion have been modified chemically to be contrary to drug delivery applications. Substitution (esterification, amidation, etherification, acetalation, etc.), chain propagation (cross-linking and/or grafting), and depolymerization are the three types of modifications that can be classified based on reaction mechanisms and functional groups (oxidation, acid, and alkali degradation) [88].

Numerous investigations have been conducted to change the structures and characteristics of pectin via depolymerization, demethoxylation, and functional group substitutions such as amidation, sulfation, and thiolation, etc. [193–196]. Pectin can be modified to vary its physical, chemical, and biological characteristics, which expands the uses for this biocompatible material [197,198]. The two primary pectin derivatives with distinct physicochemical and functional properties are amidated and low methoxypectin. [199]. For the synthesis of amidated or low methoxy pectin, chemical processes are frequently used (Fig. 7 ).

The desired physicochemical and functional qualities of pectin can be obtained by treating it with enzymes, as is well known. The two main categories of these enzymes are those that degrade HG and those that degrade RG-I. Polygalacturonase, pectin methylesterase, pectate lyase, and pectin acetyl esterase are a few examples of HG-degrading enzymes. Rhamnogalacturonan hydrolases, rhamnogalacturonan lyases, and rhamnogalacturonan acetyl esterases are a few of the RG-I degrading enzymes. Because of their affinity for various pectin chain sites, pectin chains break at various points, resulting in a variety of fragment sizes.

An enzyme that is frequently used to modify pectin is called pectin methylesterase (PME). Commercial citrus pectin can be charged with PME to de-esterify HMP (72 % DE) to the desired LMP (35 % DE) which serves as a colon-targeted delivery system for probiotic, L. casei W8. Consequently, this reduces the zeta potential to −37 mV, and Mw from 177 to 143 kDa which significantly improved encapsulation efficiency (EE) and stability (99 %) [200]. Among these, the change in zeta potential when HMP was de-esterified from 73 % to 38 % and bind with Ca2+ to encapsulate indomethacin, improved the binding ability to Ca2+ and considerably boosted the encapsulation efficiency (81 %). This lowered the release of indomethacin compared to ordinary pectin of encapsulation efficiency (55–68 %). This may be due to the modified pectin's lower zeta potential, which enhances the ionic contact between carboxyl and Ca2+ [201].

To acquire the desired functional qualities, pectin is modified using a variety of tactics such as physical, chemical, and enzymatic approaches. Physical approaches for modifying pectin, such as ultrasonic cavitation, high-pressure homogenization, microfluidization, Fenton reaction, microwave, and cold plasma, have recently been studied [202]. These physical techniques have frequently been shown to improve emulsification, antioxidant activity, and other functional qualities. Atmospheric pressure plasma is a “green” type of modification that has not been widely used for pectin modification. In order to clarify the mechanism of plasma modification, the functional features were also connected to the structural changes. With the development in “green” methods of hydrocolloid extraction and modification, the functional qualities of pectin must be examined prior to figuring out the ultimate use involving the modified pectin in food matrices.

Basak et al. modified high methoxyl apple pectin utilising a non-thermal pin-to-plate atmospheric pressure plasma reactor. With ambient air acting as the plasma generating medium, the reactor can be envisioned as a dielectric barrier discharge plasma. The steps for the experiment are as follows: for 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15 min, about 10 g of apple pectin powder was treated at 230 V with a duty cycle of 100 s and a discharge frequency of 1.1 kHz. The high voltage electrode in the reaction chamber included 88 pins that were positioned convexly to provide a uniform distribution of plasma. The pins' distance from the flat ground electrode was kept at 5 cm. On the basis of early trials, the treatment parameters where they chose. After treatment, the pectin granules were put into zip-lock polypropylene bags for later analysis.[203].

Modified citrus pectin (MCP) has immunomodulatory action and is a natural Gal-3 antagonist. MCP can effectively bind to Gal-3's carbohydrate recognition domain and competitively block Gal-3 by doing so; it can also prevent Gal-3 from being produced [204]. Potential uses for MCP include the treatment of tumors, tissue fibrosis, atherosclerosis, and other conditions [205]. However, when Christene et al. examined the therapeutic effects of MCP on OA patients, they discovered that 12 weeks of oral MCP administration had no appreciable moderating impact in comparison to an untreated sample [206]. The failure to reach the effective local drug concentration in the joint due to the avascular nature of cartilage and quick clearance via the synovium is one of the primary reasons for the ineffectiveness of MCP oral administration [207,208]. Modified citrus pectin (MCP) preparation was described by Zhang et al. The Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) solution was used to thoroughly dissolve 100 mg of MCP powder before it was filtered and sterilised using a 0.22-m aseptic filter to create the MCP solution (10 mg/mL). In order to boost the local MCP concentration in the joint and evaluate the anti-inflammatory and chondroprotective effects of MCP during the repair of AC defects, researchers set out to develop an efficient MCP delivery method [209].

The diverse properties of pectin must be taken into account when utilising pectin nanofibers for crosslinking and biological applications. The chemical structure of pectin can vary depending on the source and extraction technique. As typical examples, pectin with a high methoxyl content gels in the presence of sugar at a low pH while pectin with a low methoxy content gels in the presence of multivalent ions. As for pectin nanofibers, the diversity of pectin on the one hand presents challenges in developing a versatile crosslinking strategy, but on the other hand brings benefits of tunable material properties (e.g., mechanical strength, degradation rate, cellular responses, etc.), which may fit a wide range of biomedical applications [210]. In this study, the varied crosslinking methodologies for electrospun nanofibers of various pectins were examined, as well as the impacts of pectin structure and crosslinking method on the biomedicine-related properties of the crosslinked pectin nanofibers. Three crosslinking methods were used on the electrospun nanofibers of three representative pectins: (1) high-methoxylated pectin (HP); (2) low-methoxylated pectin (LP); and (3) low-methoxylated and amidated pectin (LAmP). For the purpose of assessing the effectiveness of crosslinking, the morphology and chemical makeup of the crosslinked pectin nanofibers were examined. The crystallisation, mechanical properties, degradation, cytotoxicity, and ability to sustain cell development of crosslinked pectin nanofibers were investigated.

It is well-known that electrostatic interaction between divalent cations like Ca2+ and galacturonate allows them to cause the gelation of pectin [211]. Ca2+ crosslinking was anticipated to have two limitations: Because there are fewer carboxylate ions available, HP has poor crosslinking ability. Additionally, considering the Ca2+-medicated ionic junction network is supported by electrostatic force, it is susceptible to collapse in physiological environments due to ion exchanges between Ca2+ and monovalent ions (such as Na+, K+) in physiological fluids [212]. Thus, covalent crosslinking was given further thought in order to address the Ca2+ crosslinked network's instability problem.

The crosslinking reagents GLU and ADH, which target the hydroxyl groups and carboxylate ions in the saccharide rings of pectin, respectively, were selected. According to Fig. 8 , inter- and intramolecular acetal bonds would form between GLU and the two neighbouring hydroxyl groups (at C-2 and C-3 position, respectively) of pectin chains inside nanofibers as well as at fiber/fiber overlapping zones by further GLU crosslinking following Ca2+ crosslinking. In comparison, additional ADH crosslinking would proceed via a formation of hydrazide linkages between the leftover carboxylate ions of GalA units. In summary, three crosslinking strategies were used on electrospun HP, LP, and LAmP nanofibers: (1) mono-crosslinking with Ca2+; (2) dual-crosslinking with Ca2+ and GLU; and (3) dual-crosslinking with Ca2+ and ADH.

A common way of pectin modification is cross-linking. Pectin gels, films, and emulsions may have improved physical properties as a result of the cross-linking process's creation of a densely connected network. Several cross-linking agents have attempted to covalently bind proteins and polyphenols to the pectin backbone. As a cross-linking agent, glutaraldehyde is frequently used. By using periodate oxidation, reductive amination, dopamine, and sodium cyanoborohydride, Popovic et al. created a novel kind of modified pectin. Dopamine-pectin microbeads with immobilised laccase were created using an enzymatic crosslinking polymerization method based on emulsions (Scheme 1 ). Comparing the immobilised laccase to the loose enzyme, it demonstrated better temperature and pH stability [213].

From Muhoza et al., crosslinking gelatin and pectin with tannic acid drastically changed the protein's secondary structure. Coacervates that have been crosslinked showed superior melting and gelling points, a higher storage modulus (G′), and stronger intermolecular bonds overall. Crosslinking also resulted in a decrease in random coil, an increase in alpha helix, and spectral shifts at different gelatin amide groups. Additionally, peppermint microcapsules with excellent encapsulation efficiency and enhanced thermal stability were created utilising gelatin and high methyl pectin coacervates that were crosslinked using tannic acid. The findings demonstrated that tannic acid can be used to enhance the gelatin and high methyl pectin coacervate qualities and be utilised to the manufacturing of microcapsules for the food and pharmaceutical industries.[214].

Crosslinking is an essential step to take in order to create a hydrogel with the necessary qualities, such as a complex micro-structure and high stability. Several methods, including chemical crosslinking, enzyme crosslinking [215], and photo-crosslinking, [216] are being used successfully. The chemical method is the most popular choice when compared to other approaches because it can be easily implemented thanks to the large diversity of chemical structures derived from the hydrogel [217]. Many substances, including glutaraldehyde, EDC/NHS, genipin, ethylene glycol diglycidyl ether, citric acid, and others, have been used with success. Chemical crosslinkers, however, may result in some applications of biomaterials for tissue engineering and regenerative medicine that are insufficient. For instance, to a certain extent, cells and tissues are cytotoxic to glutaraldehyde and ethylene glycol diglycidyl ether [218,219]. Although genipin and EDC/NHS offer greater biocompatibility, they are too expensive for use in most applications [218,219]. Citric acid requires an additional catalyst and a high temperature for the crosslinking reaction, making it an unsuitable option for injectable hydrogel.

According to Amirian et al., in-situ crosslinked hydrogel was created and is thought to be one of the best alternatives for use in clinical applications Such hydrogel exhibits little detrimental effects on the body and is also easily moldable into the right shape for irregular wounds [220–222]. Two popular methods to synthesize in-situ crosslinked hydrogels are Michael addition and Schiff base reactions. The injectable hydrogel cannot be used in the real-world clinical application because the Michael-addition reaction has a rather slow rate of reaction and typically takes >30 min. However, compared to hydrogels based on Michael addition, which take much longer to gel, Schiff-base reactions, which are carried out by the reversible condensation of amines and aldehydes, only result in the harmless by-product of water (Scheme 2 ) [222–224].

For solution-based film formation, a water-soluble biopolymer is dissolved in a solvent such as water and heated at high temperatures while stirring to generate a biopolymer solution. The obtained solution is referred to as a film-forming solution [225]. This will be placed in a flat-surfaced teflon pan/mold and dried in the oven at appropriate temperatures. The method of preparing solution-based film is easy and inexpensive. Sood et al. prepared by dissolving 5 g pectin in 100 mL of distilled water and stirring at 800 rpm for 1 h, an edible film of red pomelo peel pectin was created. Glycerol was added as a plasticizer at 30 % (v/w) on a pectin the basis and stirred for 10 min. The solution was cast on Teflon coated plates for 24 h at 40 °C, then peeled off and kept in a desiccator for testing. The majority of pectin-based edible film is cast at room temperature [226]. The drying temperature changes depending on the polymer when films are blended. To increase the toughness of solvent cast PVA/pectin blends, morphology control and ionic crosslinking of pectin domains are used. Combining synthetic water-soluble polymers like polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with natural polymers like pectin has various benefits. The mechanical characteristics of the blends, however, deteriorate because to the brittleness of pectin [227].

A twin-screw extruder is a device with two co-rotating, intermeshing screws positioned on splined shafts in a closed barrel. Extrusion based process is one of the common thermoplastic materials such as polyethylene, polypropylene, and poly (ethylene terephthalate). Nevertheless, a substantial amount of study has been conducted on the extrusion of biopolymers. Extruded maize starch combines with synthetic thermoplastic materials and other biopolymers, are some of the examples [228–230]. While casting is a practical laboratory method and there are commercial processes employing solution casting of films, nevertheless, extrusion is an obvious alternative. Extrusion is often preferred to casting as the method of fabricating films because the throughput of the process is faster and less energy is required for the removal of water [231].

Pectin is a very complex polysaccharide that is a multifunctional natural component found mostly in plant primary cell walls [232]. Commercial pectin serves as a potent emulsifier, and pectin emulsions have been employed in investigations on carotenoid bioaccessibility. Cervantes et al. shown that highly esterified pectin emulsions improve lipolysis and bile binding, which increases polar carotenoids micronization and bioaccessibility. Furthermore, pectin content influences carotenoids' bioaccessibility [233]. According to Shamsuri et al., a film forming solution is commonly used for the development of emulsion-based films. After that, a surfactant-containing oil is made by mixing the surfactant into the oil and then adding it to the above one, which is then homogenised to make a film-forming emulsion [225]. The solution is then poured into the teflon pan/glass plates and allowed to dry at elevated temperature results in the formation of film. In comparison to the control solution-based films, the emulsion-based films have a lower water vapour permeability and a larger elongation at break [225].

Mandarin peel pectin (MPP) emulsions were made with various oil phase loadings with or without β-carotene, and their emulsifying qualities, digesting capabilities, and β-carotene bioaccessibility were examined, according to Han et al. [234]. The results confirmed that MPP emulsions had a good capacity for loading carotene, but that the loading impact was significantly influenced by the kind of carrier oil, both in terms of the chain length and saturation of the oil. With a noticeably decreased FFA release rate and higher β-carotene bioaccessibility, in vitro digestion also caused an increase in the mean particle size and zeta potential of LCT oil produced emulsions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of polymeric fiber based on electrospinning and solution blowing

ce:section.ce:para: Electrospinning (ES) is one of the most extensively utilised and versatile manufacturing techniques for fibrous materials on both a laboratory and industrial scale. [235]. As an ES device, the most basic components are: A polymer solution-filled syringe (typically paired with an electric pump), a collector grounded or connected to an opposite pole voltage source, and a metal needle attached to the high voltage supply (Fig. 9a). In addition to filtration and protective materials, electrical and optical applications, sensors, nanofiber-reinforced composites, etc., the biomedical field is one of the most important electrospinning application fields. Recently, there has been increased interest in fabricating electrospun nanofibrous composites based on natural and synthetic biopolymers using the widely utilised electrospinning technology for wound dressing application. A number of notable features of electrospun mats, such as their ability to mimic the structure of the natural extracellular matrix (ECM) for improved cell attachment and proliferation, an interconnected network, a high surface area to volume ratio and surface functionality flexibilities makes them ideal for applications in tissue engineering such as wound dressings.[236].

According to Li et al., periodate oxidation, electrospinning, and ADH-mediated crosslinking of pectin hydrogel nanofibers were used to create the fibers of pectin hydrogel nanofibers at levels of 25 and 50 % oxidation. Along with the rise in the pectin hydrogel's oxidation and crosslinking levels, the stiffness and hydrophobicity of the resulting nanofiber scaffold of pectin hydrogel also rose. Additionally, gene expression analysis was used to evaluate the potential use of pectin hydrogel nanofiber scaffolds of various stiffness in stem cell development into vascular cells. The stiffer scaffold made it easier for MSCs to differentiate into vascular smooth muscle cells, while the softer fiber mat encouraged their development into endothelial cells, in accordance with real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) results. Overall, the findings show that pectin hydrogel nanofibers may be used in stem cell-based tissue engineering because they can provide mechanical cues that promote MSC differentiation into vascular cells [237].

Among nonwoven technologies, melt blowing is the most prevalent commercial process for preparing microfibers. A molten polymer is pumped via a fine capillary and pulled into fibers. In this technique, which is facilitated by a high-velocity hot gas flow that emerges co-axially with the polymer melt. On an open screen, the fibers are gathered and molded into a nonwoven web [238]. It is used in the refining, fabric, pharmacy, and pharmaceutical sectors. Solution Blow Method (SBM) is a relatively emerging technology that produces various nano-to-micron size fiber materials from different polymers, such as natural and synthetic, and their mixtures. SBM is attracting considerable interest as solution-blown nanofibers exhibit remarkable high surface-area-to-volume ratio, porous structure, and superior mechanical properties [239]. These properties make them essential for various applications such as in textiles, environmental remediation of air and water, energy storage such as supercapacitors, engineered scaffolds, drug delivery, and biosensors.

SBM is a process that produces nanofibers using pressurized gas to cause rapid stretching and evaporation of solvent from a polymeric solution jet at the outlet of the concentric nozzles system [240]. Compared to the most widely employed fiber fabrication technology, electrospinning, SBS does not require an electric field during the spinning process, minimizing its safety concerns. SBS has a more straightforward set-up and can produce nanofibers up to thirty (30) times higher than the production rate of electrospinning [241,242], which translates to low cost, short preparation time, and high yield results. These advantages render SBM more economical and safer in large-scale production to produce nanofibers [239,240].

Tissue deterioration and organ failure are anticipated to quickly rank among the most prevalent healthcare issues in developed nations as a result of the ageing population. Artificial tissues generated in the lab and targeted delivery of stem cells offer enormous promise for addressing this issue and could potentially meet the growing demand in the field of medical care. The most recent approach in the field relies on hydrogels that can stimulate and guide progenitor cells into developing new, healthy tissues. The crucial relevance of nontoxic, biocompatible, mechanically stable, tissue regenerating, low-cost, and scalable hydrogel systems has encouraged researchers to look for abundant and bioactive polymers that may meet these characteristics [243].

Tissue engineering is a sophisticated domain within the realm of reparative medicine that originated from the discipline of biomaterials research and innovation. This technique involves the integration of cells, biologically active molecules, and scaffolds to repair, enhance, and sustain the functionality of damaged tissue or organs. The objective of tissue engineering is to integrate functional constructs that offer biological support to impaired tissue or organs, facilitating their effective restoration and regeneration [244]. For instance, a variety of therapeutic approaches have been studied to treat burn wounds, where split and full skin grafts are frequently employed (Fig. 10 ). Grafting is a therapy option; however, it comes with risks of tissue/organ rejection, infection transmission, additional surgical steps, and a challenge in locating healthy donor tissues [245]. Tissue engineering currently offers a promising prospect to patients suffering from chronic and deep injuries [246]. The engineering of a tissue involves the utilization of cells, specifically reparative cells such as embryonic stem cells that contribute to the development of functional tissue. Additionally, a scaffold, which is a biomaterial that provides support for cellular growth and proliferation, is employed. Furthermore, mediators, including bioactive molecules like growth factors or cytokines, are utilised to aid the cells in assembling into properly shaped and functioning tissue [247]. In the context of clinical practices, the utilization of human cells or tissues is subject to stringent regulatory laws, whereas scaffolds (biomaterials) are exempt from such regulatory constraints. According to the American National Institute of Health, biomaterials are defined as active substances or combinations of active substances that can be derived from natural sources or synthesized, and have the potential to be utilised either partially or in their entirety for the purpose of tissue or organ replacement [248].

The “gold standard” for hydrogels, polysaccharide-based biopolymers are among the most often utilised ones in biomedical engineering [249–251]. In response, tissue engineers have suggested creating three-dimensional (3D) microenvironments that can direct embedded cells into mature tissue and integrate in vivo with defective tissues for healing [252–257]. Hydrogel-based biomaterials were developed as a result of the emphasised importance of such 3D systems [258]. These technologies can maintain the cells in a 3D environment while retaining them during the needle injection phase to maximise postinjection cell viability and enable the best tissue regeneration [259,260].
