Pectin is a naturally occurring polysaccharide which is inexpensive, edible, recyclable, nontoxic, and advantageous for use as a biomaterial [261]. In comparison to other natural polymers, documented procedures for converting pectin prepolymers into tissue engineering hydrogels are limited, keeping this topic open to further investigation. Nonetheless, various pectin cross-linking methods have been used in recent years to create hydrogels that are biocompatible and stable in physiologically relevant settings [261–266]. Many of these approaches are based on electrostatic cross-linking, which is mediated by the interaction of positively charged ions or biopolymers like chitosan with the negatively charged backbone of pectin [261–265]. Pectin, a naturally occurring polymer, was converted by Mehrali et al. into a cross-linkable hydrogel. Methacrylating pectin, which hypothesised could produce a hydrogel when combined with a photo initiator and low-level UV light, was the only basis of the methods used to achieve this. Indeed, this was the case, and they also added gelatin to the system to make it more cell-friendly [243]. This bio-engineered substance is very innovative, adaptable, and affordable biomaterial for tissue engineering applications.

Commercially available and highly promoted antimicrobial dressings are widely available for treating a variety of wounds. The selection of dressings is based on the material type, antimicrobial agent included, delivery method, and features of the wound, such as the amount of exudates, bacterial bioburden, or infection [267]. As a bio-based polymer, pectin is known for its biodegradability, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, renewability, and low cost, all of which make it suitable for usage in a wide range of applications without risk [268]. Pectin, on the other hand, lacks antibacterial properties due to a lack of the necessary functional groups. This restricts its use in situations where antimicrobial properties are necessary, such as in edible film coatings for food products, bioactive packaging materials, encapsulating matrix for functional components in food products, drug delivery or controlled release applications, tissue engineering applications, and biomedical products like wound healing dressings [269,270].

Pectin has been combined with a variety of antimicrobial substances, including bacteriocins [271], lysozymes [272], polylysine [273], natamycin [274], nicotinic, salicylic, 5-aminosalicylic and anthranicotinic (Mudarisova et al., 2020) in order to provide antimicrobial characteristics with Alovera and curcumin extracts [276]. Chitosan, a naturally occurring polymer with antibacterial activity, as well as polyphenols derived from various plants have both been employed to create chitosan/pectin films with antimicrobial capabilities [277–279]. Essential oils derived from other plants have also been employed to add antibacterial properties to pectin [280]. Incorporating these antimicrobial materials which minimises the limitation of pectin in biomedical sector.

Some studies shows that modified pectin shows antibacterial effects through different bacterial culture. According to Roza et al., the composites' antibacterial activity was assessed using the disc-diffusion method. Pure cultures of virulent infection pathogens (Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus epidermidis, and Escherichia coli) were seeded using the “solid lawn” method over a layer of meat-peptone agar that was 4 mm thick and placed in sterile Petri dishes. The investigated substances were smeared on the nutritional medium's surface no later than 5 min after inoculation. Petri dishes were then incubated in a thermostat for 24 h at 37 °C. The size of the growth delay zones of microbial cultures at the application locations of the analysed systems was estimated after incubation. And measured how much the growth of infectious agents was inhibited in contrast to the control sample, through this activity of the compounds was determined. The findings show the potential for using these materials in medical settings, which, together with the action's duration and the lack of aggression iodine naturally possesses, makes their potential use as mild antiseptic materials appealing [275].

Antioxidants are characterised as substances that, even in relatively little amounts, can stop or slow oxidation [281]. Free radicals that can damage cells can be stabilised, inactivated, or scavenged by antioxidants. Antioxidants can generally be divided into two categories: natural antioxidants and synthetic antioxidants. Natural sources, which are abundant in plants and food, are used to make antioxidants. Most of these plant-based natural antioxidants occur in the form of polyphenols, carotenoids, and vitamins [281,282]. All plant parts, including fruits, vegetables, nuts, seeds, leaves, roots, and bark, are susceptible to them [283] The primary antioxidants that aid in wound healing are phenolic molecules. They are the secondary metabolites that plants create in the greatest amounts. The most often utilised phenolic compound among all those discussed in this paper is flavonoids. These naturally occurring antioxidants exhibit a variety of biological functions, including antioxidant, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory capabilities, which makes them suitable for use as a biomaterial component for wound healing.

Natural polysaccharide pectin has attracted more attention recently because of both its biomaterial and medicinal functions. Pectin has functional properties such as gelatin with high viscosity, thickening, emulsification, and so on [284–286]. Additionally, in recent years, the biological activity of pectin has received greater attention than its physical features. Pectin, on the other hand, has a limited range of applications due to its high molecular weight and poor solubility [287–289]. The biological features of pectin are related to viscosity, molecular weight, solubility, and structure [290]. According to several studies, the effects of ultrasonic on the rates of hawthorn pectin's decomposition, its structural characteristics, and its antioxidant activity were investigated. The structure, physical and chemical characteristics, and antioxidant activity of pectin. The outcome revealed that ultrasonic processing might boost pectin's antioxidant capacity. Increased GalA content and decreased DE, turbidity, intrinsic viscosity, and molecular weight were part of the process by which ultrasound increased antioxidant activity [291].

Mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana L.) rind is one of the several sources of pectin. We examined the antioxidant activity of the pectin from the Indonesian mangosteen rind extract in this work. In comparison to ascorbic acid, this pectin has a moderate antioxidant activity. These findings indicate that the pectin from Indonesian mangosteen has significant potential for development as a biomaterial for biomedical use [292]. In a rat model, the effects of a pectin hydrogel (PVA-PT HG) containing Hippophae rhamnoides L leaf extract on wound healing were examined by Kim et al. The findings showed that HG containing H. rhamnoides L. leaf natural extracts enhanced the healing of burn or accident wounds. Progressively from HG, the active components of H. rhamnoides L. leaves were released; release was enhanced in acidic environments, such as inflammatory areas. Additionally, it speeds up wound recovery and may serve as a therapeutic covering for burn or accident wounds [293].

Nowadays, there has been an increase in the number of people with chronic wounds, which has resulted in serious health problems worldwide. In general, NO and pro-inflammatory cytokines are secreted during inflammation as a natural protective response, but prolonged and choric inflammation can be harmful and contribute to the pathogenesis of diseases like fever, asthma, atherosclerosis, arthritis, neurodegenerative diseases, and even cancer [294–296]. Anti-inflammatory medications, both steroid and non-steroid, are the core component of inflammation treatment. However, both have negative effects, such hypertension, osteoporosis, immunosuppression, cushing syndromes [297] and hemorrhagic gastritis, upper digestive ulcer, hepatotoxicity, allergies of the latter, make their long-term usage controversial and represent an important limitation to the use of these drugs [298]. Finding possibly non-toxic and efficient anti-inflammatory drugs is therefore essential.

The rate-limiting stage of chronic wound healing has been found to be the inflammation stage, and strategies for shortening the prolonged inflammatory response have proven to be effective for increasing the healing rate. Recently, various anti-inflammatory strategies (such as anti-inflammatory drugs, antioxidant, NO regulation, antibacterial, immune regulation and angiogenesis) have attracted attention as potential therapeutic pathways. Moreover, various biomaterial platforms based on anti-inflammation therapy strategies have also emerged in the spotlight as potential therapies to accelerate the repair of chronic wounds [299]. The anti-inflammatory function of hydrogel dressings has been significantly improved, addressing many clinical Challenges presented in ongoing endeavours to promote wound healing [300].

Inflammation is the immune system's initial response to damage, infection, and stress. Many diseases, including arthritis, atherosclerosis, and cancer, are linked to chronic inflammation. The standard inflammation treatment method mostly consists of steroid and non-steroid anti-inflammatory medicines, both of which have significant adverse effects that cannot be ignored. Polysaccharides derived from natural sources have received a great deal of attention in recent years due to their safety, accessibility, and significant anti-inflammatory properties, but comprehensive research into their role in anti-inflammation is still lacking. Some research has focused on the anti-inflammatory properties of pectin polysaccharides. For example, pectin polysaccharide HBHP-3 (Houttuynia cordata polysaccharide) was extracted and purified from H. cordata [301]. The primary monosaccharides in HBHP-3 were rhamnose, arabinose, glucose, galactose, and galacturonic acid, which were synthesized in various molar ratios. The results showed that HBHP-3 exhibited outstanding anti-inflammatory action, which could be attributed to its structural properties as a possible anti-inflammatory drug.

As more research demonstrates the great efficacy of modified pectin (MP), notably modified citrus pectin (MCP), in preventing the growth and spread of cancers including colon and breast cancer, it is becoming more widely used. Pectin is altered by a recently discovered chemical mechanism, which either causes or predisposes to the release of pectin molecule fragments during intestine digestion [302,303]. These fragments attach to and inhibit the action of the pro-metastatic regulatory protein galectin 3 (Gal-3) and inducing tumor progression during digestion [303].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing activity of pectin materials
ce:section.ce:para: Surgical operations, burns, diabetic ulcers, venous ulcers, trauma, and accidents all contribute to an increase in dermal injuries worldwide. A non-healing ulcer and a significant health risk factor are the result of cutaneous damage to the human body. This highlights the necessity for the development of innovative treatments in this field. Various biochemical and cellular mechanisms are involved in the complex biological process of tissue development and regeneration during wound healing. It consists of five interconnected phases: haemostasis, inflammation, migration, proliferation, and maturation [323]. These stages must be carried out sequentially with appropriate time between each for optimal wound healing. Therefore, a key component of the treatment of a wound is the use of wound healing materials to support and enhance the wound healing process by maintaining an environment that is conducive to the regrowth of the wounded tissue.

An ideal material for wound healing should be biocompatible, nontoxic, nonallergenic, able to maintain a moist environment, improve oxygen exchange, protect the wound from germs, and entrap wound exudates. Since oxygen is involved in numerous wound healing processes, such as oxidative bacterial destruction, reepithelialisation, angiogenesis, and collagen production, wound dressings with sufficient oxygen-permeability may be essential for wound healing. Due to their distinct physicochemical and biological characteristics, polyelectrolyte complexes (PECs), which are created by mixing biopolymers with opposite charges, have recently attracted a lot of attention as materials for treating wounds [324].

Cartilage often sustains damage and exhibits a restricted ability to undergo healing. The existing therapeutic options are deemed inadequate due to their tendency to generate fibrocartilage rather than hyaline cartilage, and their failure to impede the advancement of degenerative alterations [334]. Articular cartilage is a distinct form of connective tissue characterised by the absence of blood arteries, nerves, and lymphatic tissue. Due to the restricted ability of cartilage tissue to heal, the progression of focal cartilage defects results in more widespread degenerative alterations or osteoarthritis. Articular cartilage injury is a debilitating condition marked by fibrillation and subsequent loss of the articular cartilage surface [335–337]. Numerous conditions, such as trauma, arthritis, and sports-related injuries, can cause cartilage and subchondral bone damage [338–341]. According to previous reports, a significant proportion of patients who undergo knee arthroscopy, approximately 60 %, demonstrate evidence of cartilage damage. Furthermore, it has been observed that around 15 % of individuals aged 60 years and above exhibit clinical symptoms indicative of such cartilage damage [342,343].

The regenerative capacity of damaged cartilage is constrained due to its inherent deficiencies in vascularization, innervation, lymphatic networks, and progenitor cells [343–349]. In order to achieve effective materials design for bone-tissue engineering, it is necessary to possess an in-depth understanding of the composition and structure of native bone tissue. Additionally, the selection of biomimetic natural or tunable synthetic materials (commonly referred to as biomaterials), including polymers, bioceramics, metals, and composites, must be performed with extreme care. Bone tissue has the ability to heal itself, which is enough to heal small cracks and some types of fractures. However, bone defects that are larger than the critical size threshold (usually >2 cm, based on the anatomical site) will not heal on their own [350–352]. Traumatic injury, degenerative disease, congenital defects, and surgical removal of tumors can result in significant bone defects or absences that necessitate clinical intervention for functional restoration and full healing [353]. Currently, bone autografts and allografts, as well as bone fixation utilising biologically inert metallic implants, are the gold standard therapies for extensive bone lesions [354,355], with millions of such surgeries performed annually [356]. Significant advancements have been made in the domain of bone-tissue engineering, particularly in the development of materials that promote the process of bone regeneration at the site of defects, while minimizing associated risks [357]. Currently, there have been significant advancements in the field of materials synthesis and processing, accompanied by a deeper comprehension of bone biology and structure. These developments have opened up novel possibilities for the creation of increasingly intricate materials specifically tailored for bone-tissue engineering.

The ability to replace implantation surgery with a minimally invasive injection technique and the ability to shape injectable hydrogels into any desired shape, to match irregular deformities, have recently caught the attention of biomaterials scientists for applications in the engineering of cartilage and bone tissue [341,358–362]. Fig. 12 depicts the schematic explaining injectable hydrogels for use in bone and cartilage tissue engineering. The ordered structure of bone and its inherent mechanisms of formation during embryonic development and the healing of fractures serve as a source of inspiration for innovative strategies in the field of bone tissue engineering. Similar to other biological tissues, bone is composed of distinct cell types that are enveloped by an extracellular matrix (ECM). The ECM contains biologically active molecules that are either integrated within it or secreted by the cells themselves [363,364]. Bone tissue is a naturally occurring nanocomposite made up of a variety of cell types, inorganic minerals, primarily calcium phosphate, and organic proteins, primarily collagen type [365–367]. The crosslinked collagen fibrils [368] that make up the bone ECM, which is similar to a scaffold used in bone tissue engineering, self-assemble into fibers, which subsequently form sheets or bundles of fibers. Carbonated hydroxyapatite, also known as bone mineral, is composed of calcium phosphate crystals and is located within and between the collagen fibrils [369]. The mechanical properties of bone are influenced by the arrangement and combination of its structural components in a hierarchical manner. Furthermore, the structural and compositional characteristics of tissues in non-human organisms, ranging from plants to marine animals, serve as a source of inspiration for enhancing the functionalities of bone-tissue-engineering materials [370,371].

With the goal of overcoming the limitation on cartilage self-repair through the creation of cellular scaffolds that closely resemble the intricate structure of cartilage tissue, cartilage tissue engineering (CTE) is proposed as an emerging promising therapeutic strategy that offers advantages over the current treatment approaches [328,372–374]. Numerous scaffold-based techniques for cartilage repair have been studied in recent decades [375–377], and tissue engineering is now a potential strategy for cartilage regeneration [378,379]. Electrospun nanofibrous scaffolds stand out among the numerous types of scaffold materials for their distinctive structure, including a significant specific surface area, high porosity, and ECM-biomimetic structure [380], which promotes cell proliferation, migration, and differentiation.

Articular cartilage is a vital component of the body's joints, consisting of a limited number of chondrocytes (approximately 30 % by volume) and a significant number of extracellular matrix (ECM) secreted by chondrocytes.[381]. The collagenous and proteoglycan components of cartilage's extracellular matrix (ECM) can give the tissue a framework and elasticity and protect it from pressure, shear, and tear [375]. Due to the low regeneration rate induced by the lack of vasculature and low cellularity, cartilage trauma or injury frequently results in chronic and progressive degenerative joint diseases [376]. Pectin has been found to offer considerable potential in the context of bone tissue engineering. Previous research has shown that the branched portions of pectin (rhamnogalacturonan I and rhamnogalacturonan II) can be enzymatically modified to stimulate the growth and differentiation of mammalian bone cells. Kokkonen et al. extracted various pectin branching areas from apple tissues using two enzymes. Rhamnogalacturonans with shorter side chains were found to be able to stimulate local bone development and differentiation [382]. In addition to being more easily modulable than other natural polymers like alginates, pectin gels are desirable cell carriers. Other counterions (such as Nab) have the ability to rapidly remove calcium ions from the pectin egg-box structure, causing the insoluble gel to separate into soluble pectin. Pectin gels can therefore be thought of as externally programmable systems for the release of medicinal substances or immobilised cells. Additionally, the gels can stimulate the nucleation of a mineral phase on pectin microspheres, [383] resulting in the creation of biomimetic structures that better replicate the natural architecture of the bone.

Munarin et al. investigated the ability of pectin to enhance bone tissue development as a cell carrier. Through the carbodiimide chemistry, pectin was modified with RGD-containing oligopeptides and evaluated for its ability to promote cell adhesion in comparison to unmodified pectin. The modified pectin shown in Scheme 4 . Here, the use of pectin and RGD-pectin microspheres as a cell delivery system for bone tissue regeneration was proposed. The results supported the potential for LM (Low Methoxyl) pectin to be used as a new biomaterial with remarkable characteristics for bone tissue regeneration. Pectin microspheres have recently been investigated for their potential as injectable scaffolds for tissue regeneration in addition to their potential as medication delivery systems. Preosteoblast adhesion was enhanced by the chemical grafting of a peptide with the RGD sequence onto the pectin structure, promoting their metabolic activity and differentiation [331].

Mallick et al., clearly mentioned the regeneration efficacy of prepared scaffolds. Chitosan (CH), poly (l-lactide) (PLLA), and pectin (PC) compositions were tailored to generate polyelectrolyte complex-based porous scaffolds via freeze drying and crosslinked by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) solution containing chondroitin sulphate (CS) [332]. Coaxial electrospinning technology was used to successfully create PCL/GAS composite nanofibrous mats. The chitin-derived GAS (Glucosamine sulphate) was wrapped in PCL nanofibers as a stimulating material for cartilage regeneration, which was advantageous to the maintenance and sustained release of GAS. The GAS might be released slowly over 48 h with a total release amount <50 %, implying that it could be released constantly over a longer period of time. The biocompatibility of the PCL/GAS composite nanofibrous mats was greater than that of the control group after 24 and 48 h. The cell viability of rat ACs on PCL/GAS5 % and PCL/GAS10% mats were >130 % after 48 h, showing that GAS release was favourable to cell growth and proliferation and may have application possibilities in the healing of cartilage arthritis [384].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and future trends of pectin based materials
ce:section.ce:para: Polypeptides, polynucleotides, and polysaccharides are the three primary forms of polymers required for life as we know it. Biopolymers have grown in importance and popularity throughout time, whether for use in biomedical research, the food and pharmaceutical industries, or other uses. The stability, ideal molar mass, and interactions with other compounds are just a few of the issues that need to be thoroughly investigated and handled, despite the fact that their production and use are constantly growing. A wide range of domains can use biopolymers, from woven fibers that expand when in contact with water to hydrogels rich in water that can promote and maintain a humid environment, thanks to their multifunctional behaviour and stability at different physical states. In specific, there is a surge in need for a renewable strategy that incorporates drugs as well as various cell-repair proteins. The numerous favourable qualities of biopolymers are expected to be utilised in the future development and administration of these treatments, which has the potential to redefine wound healing procedures.

Pectin is a polysaccharide that can be obtained from numerous plant sources. It has numerous health advantages for individuals, including lowering cholesterol and blood sugar levels. Pectin is therefore employed in a variety of pharmaceutical and biological processes. In the meanwhile, its use as scaffolds for tissue engineering and Cartilage repair is restricted by its low mechanical strength and quick rate of deterioration. To improve these qualities, it can be changed or mixed with other organic molecules, polymers, and/or inorganic substances. The role of neutral sugar components in pectin molecules in enhancing biocompatibility in pectin-based scaffolds has to be identified in the future. It's intriguing to see that drug-encapsulated scaffolds, micro- and nano-based hydrogels, and pectin-based hydrogels are emerging as viable research areas. Due to its prebiotic impact, hypoglycemic effect, hypocholesterolaemia effect, and anti-cancer effect, pectin also possesses substantial potential. In the biomedical field, pectin polysaccharides are also anticipated to develop widely. The main issues facing humanity should be resolved as a result of their discoveries and consequences. The focus of future study will also be on biopolymers, biotechnologies, and renewable energy sources.

Flavonoid incorporated pectin can be a therapeutic potent in tissue engineering scaffolds. The cell walls of plants have the potential to serve as repositories for physiologically active polysaccharides. The pharmacodynamic capabilities of these polysaccharides have been demonstrated, indicating their potential for advancement in the realm of healthcare [385]. These entities carry out significant roles, including the regulation of growth, defense against pathogens, and adaptation to environmental stressors. Compounds derived from medicinal plants, which have non-toxic properties, have the potential to enhance the immune system in individuals with weakened immunological responses due to conditions such as cancer, AIDS, and malaria. Flavonoids are commonly observed as plant pigments across a wide range of species. These compounds are generated within the reproductive structures, specifically the flowers, fruits, leaves, and seeds, of a diverse array of plant species. The collection of these entities bestows significant ecological and physiological advantages.[386,387].

There are methods exemplify the notable advantages of biomaterials in the context of drug delivery. These advantages encompass enhanced drug bioavailability and stability, as well as the facilitation of tissue regeneration and wound healing. Modern wound dressings have made significant advancements in the development of antibacterial dressings by extracting numerous polymers and bioactive compounds from natural sources, particularly plants. These dressings encompass a wide range of materials including hydrogels, films, scaffolds, fibers, sponges, and other biomaterials. These innovative dressings have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in promoting wound healing. Biomaterials possess the ability to imitate the extracellular matrix, thereby exerting an impact on cellular behaviour, including migration and proliferation. This, in turn, fosters a moist and sterile environment for wound healing, which facilitates tissue regeneration in a synergistic manner. Numerous ongoing research are being undertaken to investigate the impact of dressings enriched with flavonoids on the process of scarless regeneration. A better understanding of the water solubility characteristics of flavonoids is crucial in order to explore the appropriate hydrophilic or hydrophobic encapsulation carriers, which can synergistically enhance drug release through crosslinking mechanisms and ultimately enhance the bioavailability of flavonoids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prospects for the use of pectin-based materials as bio-medical scaffolds as wound monitoring sensors and utilization of polymer with artificial intelligence
ce:section.ce:para: For the fifth green chemistry principle: safer solvents and auxiliaries, it's important to focus on extracting pectin without using solvents or neoteric. Similarly, structural modifications of pectin must follow the same rules as this principle. Green solvent extraction methods have yet to be implemented in the literature. There is still a knowledge gap to be filled, so new studies should focus on the use of solvents such as deep eutectic solvent (DES), natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES), and ionic liquids (ILs).

Current wound care decisions are typically based on guidelines about the type and vicinity of the wound, underlying diseases, and visual evaluation by healthcare professionals [388]. Chronic wounds are the main reason for limb amputation, yet this approach has not proven to be very successful. One of the obstacles in clinical wound care for making better decisions and personalising therapies is a lack of specific knowledge about the wound environment [389,390]. Due to their benefits, such as flexibility [391,392], stretchability [393,394], real-time monitoring, non-invasiveness, high sensitivity [395,396], and high stability [397,398], wearable electronics and intelligent patches have received a lot of attention recently. The use of biopolymers in wound treatment is one of the finest ways to treat wounds that meet all criteria without releasing hazardous compounds into the wounded area. The creation of functionalized materials with AI-formulated polymer drug release is an innovative method for creating smart materials. The use of biopolymers in sensor applications is somewhat limited. A unique technique for creating intelligent biomedical scaffolds is the use of pectin in wound care. Fig. 13 represents the schematic representation of wearable sensors with AI tools.

The incorporation of AI-advanced materials with real-time prognosis and monitoring of the condition of the injured region is currently the new trend in the biomedical industry (Fig. 14 ). Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) approaches are transforming data management, product design, materials science, and mechanical engineering, among other industrially and socially relevant domains. The Materials Genome Initiative [399] is one of the ground-breaking large-scale programmes in AI-aided material discovery, and it has sparked additional technological advances in novel materials for cutting-edge commercial applications [400,401]. Innovative hierarchical mechanical metamaterials, whose multi-scale porous and lattice structures are crucial for simulating the biomechanical characteristics of human tissues and organs, will substantially improve the biomedical field. One of the key techniques in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine is the recapitulation of the mechanical properties of the cell microenvironment through the use of artificial materials, or “scaffolds” that are implanted. [402,403].

A branch of artificial neural networks known as deep learning is used by the AI to determine the depth of laser-cut surfaces and by the AI to forecast the response of skeletal stem cells to topography. A machine learning architecture using nonlinear mathematics and powerful computing techniques that is becoming more and more common is called deep learning. This is a large part of how AI came to be known as a “black box.” A photo of a cat can be input into this mysterious black box found in popular smartphone apps, and the right tag label can be output thanks to the utilization of sizable image databases like ImageNet and Open Images [404]. Artificial neural networks are data transformation computing techniques that do not necessitate users to grasp the underlying physics or science of the transformation. They can provide statistically plausible solutions to issues that are too difficult or time-consuming to be resolved directly from fundamental concepts and current scientific knowledge since they are universal approximators [405,406]. Tissue-tissue interfaces, such as tendon-bone and cartilage-bone junctions, where structures and compositions are more complex than in single-tissue areas currently under study, could be another important area for AI integration.

On the basis of the preceding discussion, it can be concluded that pectin, which is inexpensive, biodegradable, nontoxic, and biocompatible, has emerged as a potential candidate for stabilising environmental problems caused by toxic substances. This review article we mainly focused on pectin and their extraction, functionalization, and potential applications in the biomedical field and its future demands with AI tools. These applications include its anticancer activity, antimicrobial property, wound healing and cartilage regeneration. However, because of the variability in the pectin structure, the application of pectin matrices can take many different forms. But it shows great promise in many fields related to food packaging, drug delivery, electrochemical sensors, etc. particularly in the search for active formulations. In order to have a better handle on the pectin-based products that are produced as a result of these studies, it will be necessary to conduct additional research on pectin matrices and to optimise the polymer production processes.
